parentQuestion: Nondeclarative memories preserve the circumstances in which they are learned.

scaffolds.q: The brain has multiple memory systems that work differently.
scaffolds.explain: Yes! Memory isn't one thing - declarative memory (facts and events) and nondeclarative memory (skills and habits) use different brain structures.

scaffolds.q: Declarative memory stores facts and events you can consciously recall.
scaffolds.explain: Correct! Declarative = 'you can declare it' - facts (semantic memory) and personal experiences (episodic memory) that you can talk about.

scaffolds.q: Nondeclarative memory stores skills and habits - 'how to' knowledge.
scaffolds.explain: Yes! Nondeclarative = procedural memory. Riding a bike, typing, tying shoes - skills your body knows without conscious recall.

scaffolds.q: Episodic memories include contextual details - when, where, how you felt.
scaffolds.explain: Correct! Your wedding day, graduation, that embarrassing moment - episodic memories preserve the full scene with time, place, and emotion.

scaffolds.q: Procedural memories (like how to ride a bike) do NOT preserve the circumstances of learning.
scaffolds.explain: Yes! You know HOW to ride a bike, but you probably can't recall the exact afternoon you learned. The skill remains; the context is lost.

scaffolds.q: Patient H.M. could learn new motor skills but couldn't remember the learning sessions.
scaffolds.explain: Correct! H.M. lost his hippocampus (declarative memory) but kept his procedural memory intact. He improved at tasks without any memory of practice.

scaffolds.q: Nondeclarative memories are stripped of context - you know the skill but not how you learned it.
scaffolds.explain: Yes! The cerebellum and basal nuclei store the skill pattern without the story of how you acquired it.

scaffolds.q: The statement says nondeclarative memories preserve circumstances - but that's FALSE.
scaffolds.explain: Correct! Nondeclarative memories are context-free. DECLARATIVE memories preserve circumstances, not nondeclarative ones.

scaffolds.q: The answer is FALSE - nondeclarative memories do NOT preserve learning circumstances.
scaffolds.explain: Yes! The statement confuses declarative and nondeclarative. Declarative (episodic) memories preserve context. Nondeclarative (procedural) memories store skills without context.
